stem: A carpenter is building a symmetrical triangular gable for the front of a barn. The gable sits on top of a rectangular wall that is 60 feet wide and 14 feet tall. Each sloped side of the gable makes a 53° angle with the horizontal base. The carpenter needs to know the area of the triangular gable to estimate the amount of siding material required. If tan 53° = 4/3, what is the area, in square feet, of the triangular gable?
explanation: The gable is a symmetric triangle with a full base of 60 feet. Because it is symmetric, the peak is directly above the midpoint of the base, creating two right triangles each with a base of 30 feet. Using the angle at the base: tan 53° = height / 30, so height = 30 × (4/3) = 40 feet. The area of the full triangular gable is (1/2)(base)(height) = (1/2)(60)(40) = 1,200 square feet. Note: the wall height of 14 feet is extra information not needed for this calculation.

distractorRationale.A: Uses only the half-base (30 ft) as the full base of the triangle: area = (1/2)(30)(40) = 600
distractorRationale.B: Uses the half-base and the rafter length (hypotenuse = 50) instead of the height: area = (1/2)(30)(50) = 750
distractorRationale.D: Uses the rafter length (hypotenuse = 50) in place of the height with the full base: area = (1/2)(60)(50) = 1500

imageDescription: A symmetric triangular gable sitting on top of a rectangular wall. The rectangular wall is labeled 60 ft wide and 14 ft tall. Each sloped side of the triangle makes a 53° angle with the horizontal base. A dashed vertical line from the peak to the midpoint of the base indicates the height. The half-base of 30 ft is labeled on each side of the dashed line.
